settings.title: How to Make Flossing a Habit That Sticks
settings.excerpt: The trick is not willpower, it is removing every small bit of friction between you and the floss.
settings.body:
Almost everyone knows they should floss. The gap between knowing and doing usually comes down to the floss being out of sight and out of reach when you actually brush.

We see the results of that gap every day. The decay and gum trouble that show up between teeth almost always start in the very spots a brush cannot reach, which is exactly where floss earns its keep.

Make it the easy choice

The aim is not to try harder. It is to arrange things so that flossing is the path of least resistance, sitting right where your hand already goes.

A few changes that work for our patients:

  • Keep the floss next to your toothbrush, not in a drawer.
  • Floss before you brush, while you still have the energy.
  • If string floss frustrates you, switch to picks or a water flosser instead of quitting.

Doing it imperfectly every night beats doing it perfectly twice a week. A little bleeding in the first week is common and usually settles as the gums get healthier, so do not let it put you off.

What to expect over time

Most people notice their gums feel firmer and less tender within a couple of weeks. Your hygienist will notice too, because there is simply less to scrape away at your next clean.

Bring your questions to your next visit and we will show you a technique that fits your mouth. There is no single right way, only the way you will actually keep up.
